perturbation.original_text: 2.1. Term. The initial term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date, and shall continue thereafter for a period of twelve (12) months (the "Initial Term"). The Initial Term shall automatically renew for one-month periods thereafter unless either party provides 30- days advance notice of termination, unless earlier terminated pursuant to Section 2.2 hereof. For purposes hereof, the Initial Term, together with any extension or renewal terms, shall hereinafter be collectively referred to as the "Term".
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1. Term. The initial term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date, and shall continue thereafter for a period of twelve (12) months (the "Initial Term"). The Initial Term shall automatically renew for one-month periods thereafter unless either party provides 30- days advance notice of termination, unless earlier terminated pursuant to Section 2.2 hereof. However, the term can also be extended by mutual written agreement for additional periods, regardless of the initial renewal terms. For purposes hereof, the Initial Term, together with any extension or renewal terms, shall hereinafter be collectively referred to as the "Term".
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'However, the term can also be extended by mutual written agreement for additional periods, regardless of the initial renewal terms,' we create an ambiguity in the definition of 'Term.' Section 2.1 initially defines 'Term' as the Initial Term plus one-month renewals unless terminated. The addition allows for potentially indefinite extensions outside the scope of the automatic renewal, introducing uncertainty. This contradicts the specificity in Section 2.2 regarding termination before the 'Term', as it's now unclear what 'Term' actually means.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2. Early Termination. 2.2.1. Either party may terminate this Agreement prior to expiration of the Term: (i) upon thirty (30) days prior written notice, or (ii) immediately upon written notice to the other party if: (a) the other party declares or a petition is filed in any court for insolvency or bankruptcy and such petition is not dismissed in thirty (30) days; (b) the other party reorganizes under the relevant bankruptcy act or any similar statute in such party's jurisdiction of incorporation; (c) the other party consents to the appointment of a trustee in bankruptcy or a receiver or similar entity; or (d) the Developer breaches DSS's Technology or Intellectual Property rights contained herein. 2.2.2. Upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement, (i) the Technology Development Services License granted to Developer hereunder shall immediately cease, and (ii) Developer shall immediately cease use of all proprietary technology files heretofore delivered by DSS and shall deliver to DSS all such proprietary files along with any and all Improvements completed to date by Developer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an ambiguity by stating the term can be extended by mutual agreement regardless of the initial renewal terms, which contradicts the specificity of Section 2.2 regarding termination before the term. Section 2.2 allows for termination prior to the 'expiration of the Term,' but with the added text in Section 2.1, it's unclear what constitutes the 'Term' because it could be extended indefinitely beyond the one-month renewals, thus directly conflicting with the termination clause.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Definitions. For purposes of this Agreement, the following capitalized terms shall have the meanings set forth below. "Improvements" shall mean technical improvements, modifications or enhancements relating to the Technology that are developed by the Developer pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Definitions. For purposes of this Agreement, the following capitalized terms shall have the meanings set forth below. "Improvements" shall mean technical improvements, modifications or enhancements relating to the Technology that are developed by the Developer pursuant to this Agreement. However, for purposes of royalty payments as mentioned in future agreements, 'Improvements' shall encompass only those enhancements that significantly increase the market value of the Technology, as determined solely by DSS.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause 'However, for purposes of royalty payments as mentioned in future agreements, 'Improvements' shall encompass only those enhancements that significantly increase the market value of the Technology, as determined solely by DSS' creates a contradiction. Initially, 'Improvements' is defined broadly as any technical improvements. The added clause introduces a narrower definition specifically for royalty payments, creating uncertainty as to which definition applies in contexts outside royalty calculations. This can lead to disputes over ownership and usage rights of improvements if DSS determines improvements not to add any significant increase in the market value of the Technology, then these Improvements wouldn't be subjected to any royalty payments.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1. Subject to Developer's expressly granted rights under this Agreement, Developer acknowledges and agrees that DSS shall own all right, title, and interest in and to the Technology, the Improvements, its Intellectual Property, and all future derivative works derived therefrom or developed hereunder. Developer agrees that it will not at any time (i) do or cause to be done any act or thing contesting or in any way impairing any part of such right, title and interest or (ii) represent, expressly or by implication that it has any right, title or interest in or to any of the foregoing other than as expressly set forth herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a narrower definition of 'Improvements' for royalty payments, which contradicts the broader definition used in section 3.1. Section 3.1 states DSS owns all rights to 'Improvements', implying all improvements developed by the developer, regardless of their market value. The changed text, however, limits the definition of 'Improvements' eligible for royalty payments to only those that 'significantly increase the market value', creating uncertainty about ownership and rights to other improvements, contradicting section 3.1.

perturbation.original_text: 9.6. Irreparable Damage. The parties acknowledge and agree that any material breach of this Agreement may subject the other to irreparable injury for which monetary damages may not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, in addition to any remedies otherwise available, the non-breaching party may be entitled to injunctive relief and specific performance to enforce the terms of this Agreement. The breaching party shall pay all reasonable attorney's fees and court costs, arbitration costs, and/or appeal costs incurred by the non-breaching party should it be necessary for the non-breaching party to enforce the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.6. Irreparable Damage. The parties acknowledge and agree that any material breach of this Agreement may subject the other to irreparable injury for which monetary damages may not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, in addition to any remedies otherwise available, the non-breaching party may be entitled to injunctive relief and specific performance to enforce the terms of this Agreement. However, for breaches related to missed deadlines in Schedule 1, monetary damages shall be the sole and exclusive remedy available to DSS. The breaching party shall pay all reasonable attorney's fees and court costs, arbitration costs, and/or appeal costs incurred by the non-breaching party should it be necessary for the non-breaching party to enforce the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The clause 'However, for breaches related to missed deadlines in Schedule 1, monetary damages shall be the sole and exclusive remedy available to DSS' creates an in-text contradiction in Section 9.6. The original text states that for *any* material breach, injunctive relief and specific performance are options. The added text limits remedies for breaches of Schedule 1 (deliverables) to monetary damages only, conflicting with the earlier broad statement. This leads to uncertainty about whether injunctive relief can be sought for Schedule 1 related breaches, which the original text implied was possible.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.6. Irreparable Damage. The parties acknowledge and agree that any material breach of this Agreement may subject the other to irreparable injury for which monetary damages may not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, in addition to any remedies otherwise available, the non-breaching party may be entitled to injunctive relief and specific performance to enforce the terms of this Agreement. However, for breaches related to missed deadlines in Schedule 1, monetary damages shall be the sole and exclusive remedy available to DSS. The breaching party shall pay all reasonable attorney's fees and court costs, arbitration costs, and/or appeal costs incurred by the non-breaching party should it be necessary for the non-breaching party to enforce the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction within Section 9.6. The original text allows for injunctive relief and specific performance for any material breach. The changed text limits remedies for Schedule 1 breaches to monetary damages only, conflicting with the prior broad statement that implied injunctive relief was an option. Therefore, a clear contradiction exists.
